draft.introduction: At Sophist 246a–c, the Eleatic Stranger describes a perpetual battle between materialists who insist that only bodies are real and idealists who locate true being in intelligible Forms. Plato calls this the “battle of gods and giants.”

translation.text: There appears to be a sort of battle of gods and giants going on between them, on account of their dispute about being. Some drag everything down from heaven and the unseen to earth, literally grasping rocks and trees; others defend themselves from above, from somewhere in the unseen, insisting that true being is certain intelligible and bodiless Forms.
translation.original_text: γιγαντομαχία τις αὐτοῖς διὰ τὴν ἀμφισβήτησιν περὶ τῆς οὐσίας

translator_notes.note: The “battle of gods and giants” (γιγαντομαχία) over being frames one of the perennial disputes in philosophy: whether reality is fundamentally material or intelligible. The Neoplatonists decisively sided with the “gods”—the friends of the Forms.
